{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Blackmar-Diemer Gambit (BDG)| |rd|nd| |qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|pd|= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | | |bd| | |= | | | |pl|pd| | | |= | | |nl| | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|= |rl| |bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= || }} = Blackmar-Diemer Gambit (BDG): Zeller Defense = == 3. Bf5 == Bf5 defends the pawn on e4 and develops the queenside bishop, with intentions of keeping the pawn as it is a thorn to White who can't play Nf3 or Bd3, otherwise the knight/bishop will be taken by the pawn. === Responses === White usually responds with [[Chess Opening Theory/1. d4/1...d5/2. e4/2...dxe4/3. Nc3/3...Bf5/4. f3|3. f3?!]], which attacks the e4 pawn albeit blocking the development of the knight to f3, which can be rectified after exd3. A rare option that is preferred by computers is [[Chess Opening Theory/1. d4/1...d5/2. e4/2...dxe4/3. Nc3/3...Bf5/4. g4|g4]], kicking the bishop away with the intention of creating a pawn storm with h4 and h5, forcing Black to play defensively. Other than that, other options can block pieces from developing, get kicked away by pawns, or just do nothing. == Theory Table == {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} # d4 d5 2. e4 dxe4 3. Nc3 Bf5
4 5 6 7 8
